selection_rules: Prefer focused plugins with a clear user job over broad library replicas.
Include only skill IDs present in data/skills_index.json.
Prefer skills marked supported for both Codex and Claude plugin targets, without branding the plugin names around either host.
Keep each plugin small enough to remain easy to browse, install, and invoke.
official_codex_docs_basis: Codex skills are the workflow authoring format.
Codex plugins are the installable distribution unit for reusable skills, apps, and MCP-backed workflows.
Codex shortens or omits skills from the initial list when many skills are installed, so smaller plugin surfaces improve discoverability.

candidates.name: AAS Web App Builder
candidates.audience: Frontend and full-stack developers shipping modern web apps.
candidates.why: High-demand workflow with a coherent path from UI design to React/Next.js implementation, forms, Tailwind, and SEO.

candidates.name: AAS Product Design Studio
candidates.audience: Builders who want richer UI, brand, portfolio, and visual product work.
candidates.why: Turns design from a generic skill category into a strong plugin proposition with visual direction, responsive UI, motion, and asset creation.

candidates.name: AAS Security Engineer
candidates.audience: Authorized security testing, audit, and hardening teams.
candidates.why: Security has enough depth in the catalog to deserve a standalone plugin with explicit authorized-use boundaries and testing workflows.

candidates.name: AAS Secure App Builder
candidates.audience: Application developers who want security embedded while building features.
candidates.why: Separates defensive implementation from offensive assessment, making a safer and clearer plugin for product engineering teams.

candidates.name: AAS Documents & Presentations
candidates.audience: Users creating, editing, converting, and automating office documents.
candidates.why: Combines the strongest file-format skills into a concrete productivity plugin that can be extended with app integrations.

candidates.name: AAS Data Analytics
candidates.audience: Operators, analysts, and builders working with product analytics, SQL, dashboards, and experiments.
candidates.why: Data work benefits from a repeatable toolchain: tracking, SQL, Postgres, dbt, dashboards, visualization, and experimentation.

candidates.name: AAS Agent & MCP Builder
candidates.audience: Developers building agentic apps, MCP tools, RAG systems, and evaluation loops.
candidates.why: Maps directly to plugin-based agent workflows because it can grow from skills into MCP server configuration and app/tool integrations.

candidates.name: AAS OSS Maintainer
candidates.audience: Open-source maintainers managing PRs, releases, reviews, and contributor handoffs.
candidates.why: This is one of the strongest maintainer-focused plugin opportunities: it packages repeatable repo work around review, release, changelog, and GitHub workflows.

candidates.name: AAS QA & Test Automation
candidates.audience: Engineers and QA teams writing, debugging, and stabilizing test suites.
candidates.why: Testing is a natural plugin because users need a workflow, not a single skill: plan tests, automate browsers, debug failures, and fix regressions.

candidates.name: AAS DevOps & Cloud
candidates.audience: Teams shipping infrastructure, deployments, and operational workflows.
candidates.why: Strong plugin candidate because it combines deterministic scripts, cloud patterns, deployment safety, and incident workflows.

candidates.name: AAS Marketing, SEO & Growth
candidates.audience: Founders and growth teams creating content, SEO systems, experiments, and email campaigns.
candidates.why: A clearer proposition than many separate marketing skills: plan, write, measure, test, and improve acquisition work.

candidates.name: AAS Automation Builder
candidates.audience: Teams designing reliable automations across tools, data stores, and communication platforms.
candidates.why: Works best as a plugin because app connectors and MCP configuration can turn instructions into live workflows.

candidates.name: AAS Observability IR
candidates.audience: Engineering teams monitoring systems, debugging production issues, and writing postmortems.
candidates.why: Operational work needs consistent procedure and proof gates, making it more plugin-worthy than isolated observability prompts.

candidates.name: AAS Python API Builder
candidates.audience: Python developers building APIs, services, and tests.
candidates.why: A language-specialized plugin is useful when it bundles framework guidance, async patterns, testing, and API design together.

candidates.name: AAS Mobile App Builder
candidates.audience: Mobile teams shipping Expo, React Native, Flutter, and iOS apps.
candidates.why: Mobile development has a full lifecycle in the catalog: architecture, Expo routes, distribution, CI, native platforms, and store optimization.
